title: MIR-Bench: Can Your LLM Recognize Complicated Patterns via Many-Shot In-Context Reasoning?
venue: Accepted at NeurIPS 2025 Datasets & Benchmarks Track (per arXiv comments/journal-ref metadata: "The paper is accepted at NeurIPS 2025 Datasets & Benchmarks Track, and the latest version adds modifications in camera-ready"); arXiv 2502.09933, v1 2025-02-14 through v5 2025-10-23 (camera-ready). Header note: verified title corrects a prior corpus mis-title.
claim: MIR-Bench proposes the first large-scale, many-shot (4-2048 examples) in-context pattern-recognition benchmark where an LLM predicts the output y_new = f(x_new) of an unknown function f, given n input-output examples generated by that function and diverse Python data types (Sec. 3.1, Abstract). The paper's central empirical claim is that all 21 tested LLMs (including o1-preview, o1-mini, DeepSeek-R1) fail to fully exploit many-shot context: performance generally saturates by ~256 shots (often well before the 128K context limit is reached), a phenomenon attributed to attention dispersion/distraction rather than retrieval failure or hard context limits (Sec. 4.1, Sec. 4.3, Insight 1).
novelty: The authors claim MIR-Bench is 'the first large-scale, diverse, non-compilation many-shot pattern recognition reasoning benchmark' (Sec. 1, Contributions), filling a gap between (a) few-shot (<10 example) inductive/transductive reasoning benchmarks such as ARC, WILT, DEER, KORBench, MIRAGE, and (b) many-shot ICL work that is almost entirely restricted to classification (transductive-only) tasks or retrieval-style Needle-in-a-Haystack tasks that don't require aggregating many pieces of information. Their contributions: 1) MIR-Bench itself, generated via a novel automatic pipeline that mines functions from HumanEval+/MBPP+/APPS, uses GPT-4o to write data generators (avoiding non-scalable manual/LLM-direct data generation), and thereby has 'no data leakage issues' since it does not reuse existing corpus input/output pairs (Sec. 1); 2) a diversity axis (>=2 distinct input/output types) largely absent from prior many-shot benchmarks per their own comparison Table 1 / Table 7; 3) a battery of novel empirical studies (scaling effect, inductive vs. transductive, robustness to erroneous shots, SolverLearner code-based induction, RAG, meta-shot/out-of-domain transfer) that they say prior many-shot or inductive-reasoning works have not jointly examined (Sec. 1, list of six research questions).

protocol.description: Each evaluation instance is a single LLM call: the model receives n input-output demonstration pairs (n in {4,8,16,32,64,128,256,512,1024,2048}) generated by one unknown ground-truth function f, plus one new query input x_new, and must output y_new = f(x_new) in one forward response (Sec. 3.1: 'the LLMs' input will be [c1, str(x1), str(y1), ..., str(xn), str(yn), c2, x_new]'). There is no multi-turn/interactive loop in the main protocol -- it is single-shot at the call level, though the demo COUNT is the key manipulated variable (many-shot ICL), not a conversation. LLMs 'can output arbitrary rationale; however, they must end their answer with str(y_new)' (Sec. 3.1), extracted via rule-based parsing (Appendix B.6) and scored by exact match. One partial exception: Appendix B.5's difficulty-labeling procedure uses an explicit 'multi-round conversation framework with self-reflection' (round 1: guess without code; round 2: reveal ground truth, self-reflect, rate difficulty) -- but this is a meta-analysis tool for curating MIR-Core, not part of the main pattern-recognition evaluation protocol.
protocol.observation_content: Verbatim main-results prompt (Appendix B.1): "You are given some function that takes something as input and output something. You need to predict the output for the target input of that function. Remember always end your answer with 'Output: your answer', with your answer in strict python format. Here are some examples: Input: <<example input 1>> Output: <<example output 1>> Input: <<example input 2>> Output: <<example output 2>> ... (omitting more shots) Input: <<target input>>" -- i.e. the model sees ALL n demonstration input-output pairs (not partial/incremental feedback) plus the one target input, in a single prompt, and must produce one final answer. Variant prompts used for ablations (Appendix B.2): 'forced CoT' prompt adds "You need to first analyze it after 'Analysis:', then give your answer after 'Output:'"; 'no CoT' prompt adds "DO NOT OUTPUT ANYTHING ELSE INCLUDING YOUR THOUGHTS"; 'aware-error'/'aware-ratio' prompts add warnings that some shots are wrong (with or without the exact error count). SolverLearner variant (Appendix B.8) instead asks for python code implementing the function, executed externally against the query.

representations: Inputs/outputs are Python objects (x is a dict of variable name -> list/tuple/dict/number; y is any list/tuple/dict/number) rendered via str() with 'Input: '/'Output: ' prefixes (Sec. 3.1, footnote 2). The paper does not run a systematic ablation across alternative serialization formats (e.g., JSON vs. Python repr, table vs. inline) -- format is fixed across the main experiments. The main representational/format-like variable manipulated is NUMBER OF SHOTS (4 to 2048), which is the paper's core independent variable and swings accuracy substantially (Fig. 2, Sec. 4.1-4.2): performance rises with shots up to a saturation point (often ~256) then plateaus or declines. A second representational manipulation is CoT-vs-no-CoT (forced CoT / no CoT / forced nonsense, Sec. 4.4, Appendix B.2) which materially changes accuracy: no-CoT (transductive) prompting is 'unanimously and significantly higher' accuracy than forced-CoT (inductive) across all 21 models tested (Table 2). A third is duplicated-shot repetition (Sec. 4.3) to disentangle retrieval vs. aggregation. A fourth is RAG-selected vs. randomly-selected 64-shot subsets from a larger pool (Sec. 4.7, Table 5), which the paper finds makes no significant difference. A fifth is out-of-domain 'meta-shot' CoT exemplars prepended before the target problem (Sec. 4.8, Table 6), which only marginally and inconsistently helps.

models.name: o1-mini-0912
models.key_results: Best model on MIR-Extended, but 'only reaches an accuracy of less than 0.7' (Sec. 4.1, Fig. 2a). On MIR-Core (Table 2): accuracy w/ CoT = 0.334 (2.54% of answers show CoT), accuracy w/o CoT = 0.696 (93.63% of answers) -- one of the largest inductive-vs-transductive gaps of any model tested.

models.name: o1-preview-0912
models.key_results: Clearly outperforms all non-o1 models on MIR-Extended along with o1-mini (Sec. 4.1). On MIR-Core (Table 2): accuracy w/ CoT = 0.588 (56.71% of answers), accuracy w/o CoT = 0.797 (40.88% of answers) -- highest no-CoT accuracy among the 10 representative models shown.

models.name: GPT-4o-0806
strategies: RAG vs random shot selection at 128-2048 shots
used as one of 5 models for MIR-Core's D-metric factor analysis
models.key_results: Follows o1-mini/o1-preview and Claude-3.5-Sonnet in overall ranking; 'only achieve[s] less than 0.4 accuracy' among models similar to it on MIR-Extended (Sec. 4.1). Table 2: acc. w/ CoT = 0.488 (10.85% of answers), acc. w/o CoT = 0.540 (88.34% of answers). SolverLearner (Table 3): Acc@16=0.530(+0.012), Acc@64=0.534(-0.033), Acc@256=0.538(-0.029), Acc@1024=0.556(+0.004) vs. standard prompting. RAG (Table 5) shows no consistent gain over random selection (e.g., 128-shot: RAG 51.77 vs random 50.40; 2048-shot: RAG 50.53 vs random 51.63).

models.name: Claude-3.5-Sonnet
models.key_results: Ranked just behind o1-mini/o1-preview on MIR-Extended (Sec. 4.1). Table 2: acc. w/ CoT = 0.585 (98.73% of answers -- almost always uses CoT), acc. w/o CoT = 0.775 (only 1.08% of answers) -- among the largest CoT-usage rates and largest transductive advantage.

models.name: Claude-3.5-Haiku
models.key_results: 'Achieves surprisingly low accuracy' on MIR-Extended; manual inspection found 'the model often do[es] not understand our prompt and see[s] the target input as part of an incomplete data, thus refusing to answer the problem' (Sec. 4.1). Exhibits atypical behavior in the erroneous-shots robustness study, being one of two models (with Gemini-2.0 Flash) with notable deviation in the aware-vs-unaware conditions (Sec. 4.5).

models.name: DeepSeek-R1
models.key_results: Table 2: acc. w/ CoT = 0.298 (9.69% of answers), acc. w/o CoT = 0.757 (85.85% of answers) -- one of the largest inductive/transductive accuracy gaps. SolverLearner (Table 3/4): Acc@16=0.756(+0.022) up to Acc@1024=0.640(+0.003), but with high runtime-error rates that increase with shot count (RE@1024=0.2061, Table 4) -- the highest error rate of any tested model at 1024 shots.

models.name: Mistral-Large-2
strategies: used in MIR-Core D-metric factor analysis
models.key_results: Weakest/lowest 'saturation point' among models in the duplicated-shots ablation, showing 'almost not the case' for gains from genuine information aggregation (Sec. 4.3). SolverLearner Table 3/4: strong relative gains at low shots (Acc@16=0.420,+0.057) but severe degradation at scale (Acc@1024=0.356,+0.102 vs standard, with DNF@1024=0.1163, RE@1024=0.0473) -- context length exceeds its 'effective' long-context ability.

headline_numbers: Best model (o1-mini-0912) reaches <0.7 accuracy on MIR-Extended; most models (e.g., GPT-4o-0806) stay below 0.4 accuracy -- no model saturates the benchmark (Sec. 4.1, Fig. 2a).

Performance growth from more shots 'often stops at no more than 256 shots' for most models even when the 128K context limit is not yet reached, attributed to attention dispersion rather than retrieval failure (Sec. 4.1; Insight 1 confirmed via duplicated-shots ablation, Sec. 4.3).

Across all 21 models tested, no-CoT (transductive) answers have 'unanimously and significantly higher accuracy' than CoT (inductive) answers on MIR-Core, e.g. o1-mini: 0.696 (no CoT) vs 0.334 (CoT); DeepSeek-R1: 0.757 vs 0.298 (Table 2, Sec. 4.4, Insight 2).

LLMs are 'generally quite robust against erroneous shots': performance is 'only slightly harmed below 1/8' error rate and 'can maintain decent performance even with 3/4 error rate' (Sec. 4.5, Insight 3).

SolverLearner (write-code-then-execute) does not reliably beat standard prompting: 'the effect of SolverLearner varies from model to model... and does not necessarily perform better'; SolverLearner curves are 'more flat' under many-shot, underutilizing extra shots (Table 3/4, Sec. 4.6, Insight 4).
Embedding-based RAG (selecting the 64 most-similar shots by cosine similarity vs. random selection) shows 'no significant performance difference' (Table 5, Sec. 4.7, Insight 5).
Out-of-domain 'meta-shot' CoT exemplars only give inconsistent, small gains, and the benefit weakens with more in-distribution shots (Table 6, Sec. 4.8, Insight 6).

Benchmark scale: MIR-Extended = 693 functions x 10 test cases = 6930 problems; MIR-Core = 300 functions x 10 test cases = 3000 problems, selected via a fitted quadratic-difference metric D over ground-truth function complexity, answer diversity, and input complexity (Sec. 3.2, Sec. 4.2, Eq. 1, Fig. 3).
failure_modes: Claude-3.5-Haiku frequently misreads the demonstration block as 'incomplete data' and refuses to answer the target query, producing anomalously low accuracy relative to its family (Sec. 4.1).

Performance saturation/decline beyond ~256-512 shots is attributed to attention dispersion / distraction across many examples, not information-retrieval failure -- confirmed because duplicating a single informative shot to reach high nominal shot counts does not recover the performance lost at very high shot counts (Sec. 4.3, Insight 1).

LLMs systematically underestimate the true difficulty of inductive-reasoning problems when shown the (simple) ground-truth function code, e.g., GPT-4o-0806/GPT-4o-latest rate a one-line index-modulo-divisibility filter function as difficulty ~5-7/10 despite it producing 0% accuracy across all five factor-analysis models at all shot counts tested; this required a two-round self-reflection protocol to correct (Sec. 4.2, Appendix B.5).

CoT/inductive reasoning underperforms no-CoT/transductive reasoning for most models; the authors hypothesize this is because 'a mapping from input to CoT makes the equivalent of gradient descent much more opaque, thus breaking the structural coherence that allows LLMs to implicitly regress through its attention matrix' (Sec. 4.4). This gap widens with more shots.

SolverLearner (code-writing) induces high Do-Not-Finish/Runtime-Error rates that increase with shot count, especially for DeepSeek-R1 (RE@1024=0.2061) and Mistral-Large-2 (DNF@1024=0.1163, RE@1024=0.0473) as context length approaches/exceeds effective limits (Table 4, Sec. 4.6).

Context-limit truncation: for non-Gemini models, performance drop above 512 shots is 'partly attributed to exceeding the 128K context limit,' though this affects <=1% of cases at 1024 shots and is more common at 2048 shots (Sec. 4.1, footnote 3, Table 12).
one_shot_vs_multistep: MIR-Bench's main protocol is entirely single-forward-pass per query (one call sees all n demos + the target input and must emit one final answer, Sec. 3.1); there is no multi-turn interactive evaluation loop, no per-turn feedback, and no verifier-in-the-loop during scoring (scoring happens after the fact via exact match, Appendix B.6). The paper's central 'more demonstrations' lever is NOT an interactive loop but simply increasing n (the shot count) within that single call. The paper's conclusion is that increasing demonstration count alone does not translate into proportionally increasing capability: performance 'saturates' (stops improving, sometimes declines) well before context limits are reached, because of attention dispersion in aggregating many pieces of information, not because models cannot retrieve the right information (Sec. 4.1-4.3, Insight 1). Separately, the paper's CoT ablation shows that forcing the model to write out its inductive reasoning in one forward pass (forced CoT) generally hurts relative to direct transductive answering (no CoT) for most models except long-CoT 'thinking' models (o1 series, DeepSeek-R1), and this CoT benefit 'weakens with more shots,' i.e., 'CoT [has] yet to scale with many-shots to integrate thousands of pieces of information together' (Sec. 4.4, Insight 2). The paper does not test an interactive/multi-turn version of the main pattern-recognition task itself (the only multi-round conversation used anywhere in the paper is the separate difficulty-labeling meta-procedure in Appendix B.5, which is not part of the scored benchmark).

relation_to_candidate.position: MIR-Bench sits on the single-shot side of the protocol axis: each scored instance is exactly one LLM call observing ALL demonstrations plus one query input and emitting one final answer (Sec. 3.1), with no interactive/multi-turn loop, no per-turn feedback, and no model-driven probing of additional inputs. The output shape is a single derived value/object (y_new = f(x_new), a Python list/tuple/dict/number rendered as a string) scored by strict exact match with rule-based extraction (Sec. 3.1, Appendix B.6) -- structurally the same shape as c23's single transformed short string or 4-way multiple-choice answer, not a full trajectory or multi-step structure. The verifier is an independent, deterministic ground-truth Python function executed once per instance to generate labels ahead of time (Sec. 3.2, 'Output generation'); scoring itself is then just string/object exact-match, not a live oracle in the loop. What is 'observed' by the model is the full set of n demonstrations plus the query, i.e., MIR-Bench's key manipulated axis is DEMO COUNT (4 to 2048), not partial/incremental information revealed across turns.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both MIR-Bench and c23 are inductive/transductive pattern-recognition tasks that define an unknown/latent underlying function or rule set, generate input-output demonstration pairs from it, and require the model to predict the output for a held-out query in one forward pass, scored by exact match against an independently computed ground truth. Both are explicitly contamination-resistant by construction: MIR-Bench mines seed functions from coding benchmarks but generates fresh input-output shots via custom data generators rather than reusing existing corpus text (Sec. 1, 'no data leakage issues'); c23 uses invented vocabularies and randomized rule subsets for the same purpose. Both treat the number of few-shot demonstrations as a live, meaningful independent variable that changes accuracy substantially. The paper's related-work section explicitly discusses the same neighboring lineage c23 draws on: mini-SCAN/SCAN-style compositional induction (Qiu et al.), ARC/1D-ARC variants, List Functions, Programming-by-Examples -- MIR-Bench's own Table 7 comparison directly lists Mini-SCAN, ARC, 1D-ARC, List functions, and SyGus as prior pattern-recognition benchmarks, the same family c23 draws its literature parents from.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Demo-count regime: MIR-Bench's headline manipulation is MANY-shot (up to 2048 examples) explicitly targeting long-context/attention-aggregation limits; c23 is a few-shot ladder (a handful of demos) targeting whether a model states/induces K independent, nameable rules, not raw context-scaling. (2) Underlying rule structure: MIR-Bench's ground-truth functions are arbitrary Python programs mined from coding benchmarks (of highly heterogeneous, uncontrolled complexity/topic) with no guarantee of decomposing into K independent, separately-scoreable sub-rules; c23 is deliberately engineered so the rule set decomposes into K independent, inspectable strata (casing-by-length, positional swaps, ISL/OSL edits, command-to-action mappings, 1D-ARC transforms) that give a smooth incremental difficulty ladder with a stated ceiling (state all rules) and floor (naive prompt) -- MIR-Bench has no equivalent 'partial rule credit' structure; each MIR-Bench instance is single monolithic-function induction, all-or-nothing on one exact match. (3) Domain/representation: MIR-Bench's inputs/outputs are arbitrary Python data structures (lists, dicts, numbers) across highly diverse function-derived domains; c23 is scoped specifically to short-string transformations over small invented token vocabularies. (4) Multiple-choice option: c23 offers a 4-way MC variant (each distractor wrong in exactly one rule) as an alternative to free-form output; MIR-Bench has no MC variant -- it is always free-form exact match. (5) Scale/cost: MIR-Bench evaluates 21 LLMs across 10 shot-counts on up to 6930 (MIR-Extended) or 3000 (MIR-Core) problems, a much larger and more expensive design than a quick-test rubric would need.
relation_to_candidate.what_c23_would_add: Beyond MIR-Bench, c23 would add: (a) a genuinely decomposable K-independent-rule structure with an explicit incremental ladder (partial prompt quality maps to how many rules are stated/induced), letting a quick-test resolve WHICH rule strata a model misses rather than MIR-Bench's single monolithic pass/fail per arbitrary function; (b) a 4-way multiple-choice scoring option with distractors wrong in exactly one rule, giving a second, lower-variance signal MIR-Bench does not offer; (c) a tightly-scoped string-transform domain (vs. MIR-Bench's arbitrary heterogeneous Python function domain), trading MIR-Bench's diversity for reproducible, small, cheap instances suited to rapid quick-testing rather than long-context stress-testing; (d) deliberate exclusion of the many-shot/long-context axis MIR-Bench is built around, since c23's few-shot demos are meant to be the ONLY channel for latent rules (by design, not as a scaling stress test) -- so c23 is a complementary, cheaper, more surgical instrument for diagnosing rule-induction failure at the K-rule-composition granularity, whereas MIR-Bench diagnoses failure at the many-shot-aggregation-capacity granularity.

corpus_claim: MIR-Bench (Yan et al., NeurIPS 2025 D&B) tests many-shot in-context inductive reasoning over function-based I/O pairs and is c23's demo-count / many-shot lever. (The corpus previously mis-titled this paper; the verified title is 'MIR-Bench: Can Your LLM Recognize Complicated Patterns via Many-Shot In-Context Reasoning?')
corpus_claim_verdict: supported, with the title correction already folded into the claim as written. The paper's own abstract and Sec. 3.1 confirm it tests 'many-shot In-Context Learning' pattern recognition where 'LLMs are given examples of input-output examples generated by an underlying unknown function' and must predict output for new input -- i.e., function-based I/O pairs, exactly as claimed. The 'demo-count / many-shot lever' framing is also directly supported: the paper's central independent variable is the number of shots (4 to 2048, Sec. 4.1) and its central finding is about saturation/non-scaling of that lever (Insight 1). The venue (NeurIPS 2025 Datasets & Benchmarks Track) and the corrected title are both independently verified from the arXiv header metadata (per the paper's own extraction-quality header notes). No discrepancy found between the corpus claim as given and the paper's content.
baseline_relevant: Shot-count dial used: {4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048} shots, with each larger-shot test case guaranteed to be a strict superset of smaller-shot test cases (to isolate the effect of added information) except in the erroneous-shots ablation (Sec. 4.1). Useful reference for how many few-shot demo counts to test if building a demo-count axis into a c23-style baseline.

Prompt format that swung accuracy: no-CoT vs forced-CoT prompting produces large, consistent swings (Table 2: e.g. o1-mini 0.696 vs 0.334; DeepSeek-R1 0.757 vs 0.298) -- suggests a c23 baseline should fix or explicitly test the CoT-vs-direct-answer axis since it is a major confound, not just demo count or rule count.

Metric: strict exact-match accuracy via rule-based answer extraction (find last 'Output:'/'output:' occurrence, strip markdown artifacts, then compare string or ast.literal_eval'd Python object against ground truth string; special-cased for single-key dict / single-element set wrapping) (Appendix B.6) -- directly analogous to c23's exact-match scoring and a concrete reference implementation pattern for answer extraction robustness.

Floors/ceilings observed: best model (o1-mini) < 0.7 accuracy on MIR-Extended; most models < 0.4 (Sec. 4.1) -- no model saturates, meaning headroom exists but ceiling reachability is a real risk (directly relevant to c23's own noted risk that InductionBench found frontier models fail even the simplest complexity class). MIR-Bench's problem-selection pipeline explicitly filtered out problems that are 0%-accuracy for ALL FIVE factor-analysis models across ALL TEN shot counts (Sec. 3.2, 'Prompt building') -- i.e., they actively excluded unreachable-ceiling instances, a concrete precedent for c23's own 'calibrate reachability before freezing' implementation step.

Instance/problem sizes: MIR-Extended = 693 functions x 10 test cases = 6930 problems; MIR-Core = 300 functions x 10 test cases = 3000 problems (curated from MIR-Extended via a fitted difficulty-growth metric D, Eq. 1, Sec. 4.2) -- a reference scale for how large a many-shot-oriented benchmark needs to be to support robust model comparison, though much larger than what a quick-test would need.

Data/contamination status: benchmark functions are sourced from HumanEval+/MBPP+/APPS but fresh input-output shots are generated via custom GPT-4o-written data generators rather than reusing the benchmarks' own test data, explicitly to avoid 'data leakage issues' (Sec. 1, Sec. 3.2) -- a directly relevant precedent for how c23 should think about deriving fresh, generator-native instances even when literature parents (InductionBench) provide the underlying rule taxonomy.

Robustness dial: erroneous-shot injection at ratios {1/64, 1/32, 1/16, 1/8, 1/4, 1/2, 3/4} at {64, 256, 1024} shots (Sec. 4.5, Appendix B.7) -- found models 'quite robust' even at 3/4 error rate, a useful calibration point if c23 considers a noisy-demonstration difficulty dial.

Difficulty-labeling caution: LLM-assigned difficulty scores for inductive-reasoning problems are unreliable when the model sees only the ground-truth CODE (tends to underestimate), requiring a two-round self-reflection protocol (guess first, then reveal answer and self-rate) to get accurate difficulty labels (Appendix B.5) -- directly relevant if c23's implementation plan uses any LLM-assisted difficulty curation for its rule strata.
quotes: "we propose MIR-Bench, the first many-shot in-context reasoning benchmark for pattern recognition that asks LLM to predict output via input-output examples from underlying functions with diverse data format" (Abstract).

"the LLMs' input will be [c1, str(x1), str(y1), str(x2), str(y2), ..., str(xn), str(yn), c2, x_new]... LLMs can output arbitrary rationale; however, they must end their answer with str(y_new)... The answer is extracted with rule-based scripts, and exact match will be performed to determine the LLM's performance in accuracy" (Sec. 3.1).

"the best model, o1-mini-0912, only reaches an accuracy of less than 0.7, while most models such as GPT-4o-0806 only achieve less than 0.4 accuracy... Claude-3.5-Haiku achieves surprisingly low accuracy; upon checking examples, we find that the model often do not understand our prompt and see the target input as part of an incomplete data, thus refusing to answer the problem" (Sec. 4.1).
"Insight 1: The saturation of many-shots does not come from information retrieval, but from distraction when aggregating too many information" (Sec. 4.3).

"in all 21 models, including long thinking models such as o1, answers without CoT (i.e. transductive results) have unanimously and significantly higher accuracy than those with CoT (i.e. inductive results)" (Sec. 4.4).
"Insight 3: LLMs are quite robust against errorneous shots in many-shot inductive reasoning tasks" (Sec. 4.5).
"The result shows no significant performance difference between RAG and randomly selecting shots, thus disproving the effectiveness of embedding-based RAG" (Sec. 4.7).

"You are given some function that takes something as input and output something. You need to predict the output for the target input of that function. Remember always end your answer with 'Output: your answer', with your answer in strict python format." (Appendix B.1, main-results prompt).
